explanation: The provided requirement is that every DM must send its local matrix to other DMs with a period of 1 time unit, and that each DM is able to receive and elaborate up to 49 matrices within 1 time unit. Additionally, each DU is to update its navigation data every second. The strongest, most basic translation for periodic communication is to guarantee that, for example, the matrix update between any two DMs (e.g., from DM1 to DM2, DM3, DM4, DM5) occurs at least every time unit. This can be captured using the formula G(F[0,1] matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2), G(F[0,1] matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3), etc. However, the 49 matrices/period restriction is a system bound rather than a property expressible as a state formula. Focusing on the send frequency, template 70 (G (F[0,N_DURATION1] bool_exp3)) fits best to capture the periodicity of sending updates across all DM channels. We can encode this with (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 | matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 | matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 | matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5), specifying that at least one of these matrix updates is sent in every 1 time unit. N_DURATION1 is thus set to 1 to enforce the 1 time unit period.

This LTL property ensures that, within every period of 1 time unit, matrix updates are exchanged between all DM pairs. Specifically, each DM (represented by DM1 here) sends its matrix to all other DMs (DM2–DM5) and also receives matrix updates from them, meaning that up to 49 matrices can be transferred among DMs within 1 time unit. The property F[0,1] (...) U[0,1] (...) reqires that, within every interval of 1 unit, all the matrix update sending and receiving events occur, reflecting the required transmission frequency and update capability. This directly encodes the requirement that all DMs exchange their matrix states at least once every second (time unit) with all others, as expressed in the natural language.
bool_exp3: matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5

explanation: This LTL property ensures that, once every time unit (period=1), the matrix update sent from DM1 to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5 all occur (i.e., the local instance of the matrix is transmitted from DM1 to all other DMs at least once per time unit). This captures the requirement that each DM must be updated within this period. The template chosen uses 'Globally, in every interval of 1 time unit, the required property holds' for all updates from DM1 to other DMs, thus modeling the periodicity with which matrix updates are sent and, by composition, ensuring every DU (connected via a DM) receives the update every second (or time unit).

The property requires that, for every time unit (here, every second), DM1 receives matrix updates from DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5. This captures the requirement that each DM (such as DM1) is able to receive and process up to 4 (or, as per the example for all neighbors, up to 49 in a different context) matrix updates in one time unit, with the sending happening every second. Choosing F[0,1] for each update expresses that the update from each DM must occur at least once within every period of one time unit. This is enforced globally for all time intervals, so the formula guarantees periodic reception of matrix updates by DM1 in accordance with the problem statement. Other DMs are symmetric, which can be captured by the same formula applied per DM or by universal quantification in a full specification.

This formula encodes the periodicity requirement: for each generic DM, within every time unit (interpreted as 1 second), there must be an update sent from every other DM (up to 49, as the problem states the DM can receive/elaborate up to 49 matrices per time unit). Since atomic propositions for all possible matrix sends are not enumerated, we model the timing on a fixed example where DM1 receives matrix updates from DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5 as representatives. The formula G(F[0,1] (...)) asserts that in every window of length 1 time unit, all specified matrix update receipts occur, ensuring timely and periodic matrix data exchange. This captures the essential periodic update requirement discussed in the natural language input.

The requirement states that each Data Module (DM) must transmit its local matrix to the other DMs every 1 time unit, ensuring all DMs are able to receive and process up to 49 matrices within this period. Since the atomic propositions include sending individual matrix updates from DM1 to each other DM, this property ensures that for each 1 time unit, the matrix update must be sent from DM1 to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5 (to generalize, as representatives of the update pattern), and similarly, must be received by DM1 from DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5. Here, we express with a global temporal logic that in every interval of length 1, the send and receive properties must hold, matching the periodic update and transmission/processing requirement. We use the 'Globally in interval' operator with N_DURATION1=1 to capture the enforcement of these events in every 1 time unit.
bool_exp3: matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5

explanation: The natural language specifies that each DM (Distributed Module) must send its local instance of matrix M to other DMs every period of 1 time unit, and each DM can receive and process up to 49 such matrices in 1 time unit. The most direct translation using the available atomic propositions is to ensure that, within every time unit (i.e., duration 1), each DM receives matrix updates sent from every other DM and is able to perform an update. To capture the main requirement that every DM receives an update from every other DM every second, we use the atomic propositions 'matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2', 'matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3', 'matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4', and 'matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5' for DM1; a similar pattern holds for other DMs, but the proposition set only lists DM1. To cover the general property, we assert that within every 1 time unit, these propositions become true, i.e., DM1 receives the updates from all others each time unit. The LTL template that matches is the one that says 'within every N_DURATION1 time units, bool_exp3 holds', which here will be the conjunction of all required receive events. This matches LTL template 41.

The requirement states that any Data Management (DM) component must transmit its local matrix instance to all other DMs with a period of 1 time unit, and be able to receive (and elaborate) up to 49 matrices in 1 time unit. Since the atomic propositions do not specify 'broadcast to all', but there are specific propositions for matrix updates between pairs (e.g., matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2), and the central requirement is periodic updating (frequency of once per time unit), we focus on ensuring that within every time unit, each DM receives updates from the others. We choose the template that requires, globally, that in every interval of length 1 time unit, each relevant matrix update is received – this corresponds to G(F[0,1] (matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & ...)), but since there are only atomic props for DM1 receiving from others, we model the requirement for DM1 as an example. (Similar properties must exist for other DMs if their props exist.) Thus: 'Globally, in every time window of length 1, DM1 receives matrix updates from DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5.'

The requirement specifies that each DM must receive matrix updates from all other DMs every 1 time unit. The best LTL pattern to capture the strict periodicity (each DM has to get updates from all the others in each time unit) is to state that, globally, in every interval of 1 time unit, DM1 receives matrix updates from DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5. This is expressed with the F[0,1] temporal operator, and since the chosen template includes bool_exp3, which must be a valid expression, we set it to TRUE (i.e., no constraint from bool_exp3 in this scenario): F[0,1] (matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5). This ensures that within every 1 time unit, all these matrix updates are received, which matches the requirement for periodic updates and the capacity constraint.

This property expresses that in every (1 time unit) interval, for each DM, it will receive the matrix update from all the other DMs. The key idea is that if each DM transmits its local matrix instance with a period of 1 time unit, every other DM will always receive at most 4 updates (one per peer DM) in that interval, and if a DM can process up to 49 matrices in 1 time unit, this suffices. We build bool_exp3 as the conjunction (AND) of all possible matrix updates received by DM1 from DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5, and require that this holds in every rolling 1-time-unit window. We use template 41: F[0,1] (matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5), which captures that, within every 1-time-unit interval, DM1 receives matrix updates from all peers; this can be generalized for all DMs in a larger model, but with provided atomic props, we show for DM1 as representative. N_DURATION1 is set to 1 (periodicity requirement).
